CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well structured and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology and experiments (datasets, baselines, metrics) are described clearly and writing quality is good. Presentation is weakened by omission of hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, which impairs reproducibility, and the per-candidate-pair training scheme and computational costs are not clearly analysed. Figures/tables and notation appear effective from the summary, and limitations are explicitly stated, so only minor presentation/reproducibility issues prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro→method→experiments→conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early, with readable writing and a thorough related-work section. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to follow the proposed NBT variants and training procedure, and experimental claims (stronger gains on noisy ASR/DSTC2, weaker on clean WOZ 2.0) are clearly presented. Minor presentation issues hamper full reproducibility: hyperparameter details are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the limited context-modelling assumption could be highlighted more prominently in figures/tables or examples.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well structured and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology and experiments (datasets, baselines, metrics) are described clearly and writing quality is good. Presentation is weakened by omission of hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, which impairs reproducibility, and the per-candidate-pair training scheme and computational costs are not clearly analysed. Figures/tables and notation appear effective from the summary, and limitations are explicitly stated, so only minor presentation/reproducibility issues prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro→method→experiments→conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early, with readable writing and a thorough related-work section. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to follow the proposed NBT variants and training procedure, and experimental claims (stronger gains on noisy ASR/DSTC2, weaker on clean WOZ 2.0) are clearly presented. Minor presentation issues hamper full reproducibility: hyperparameter details are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the limited context-modelling assumption could be highlighted more prominently in figures/tables or examples.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important dialogue-state-tracking problem, uses standard datasets and metrics, and is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue. I award a 4 because, while the empirical contributions and practical relevance are strong and align with community expectations, the lack of principled analysis, key ablations, complexity/scalability discussion, and some experimental details prevents a full score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (dialogue state tracking) that is squarely within top-tier venue scope and targets the right audience using contemporary neural methods and standard datasets (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) and metrics. However, from my theory-focused perspective it lacks principled analysis and important ablations (e.g. gating, candidate-pair training, specialised vectors) and omits complexity/scalability analysis and hyperparameter details, which are expected for a top conference. The empirical comparison to strong baselines is appropriate but would benefit from more extensive ablations and statistical tests to fully convince this community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles dialogue state tracking with a modern neural approach, compares against strong baselines on standard benchmarks (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0), and uses the community-standard joint goal and request accuracy metrics. The focus on data-driven belief tracking that removes hand-crafted SLU lexicons and shows empirical robustness to noisy ASR (with gains from specialised embeddings like Paragram-SL999) addresses practical, reproducible concerns I value as a practitioner. Methodology and evaluation align with venue expectations and the positioning relative to prior work is appropriate for the dialogue and NLP research community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing NBT matches or outperforms delexicalisation baselines and benefits from semantically specialised vectors. I award a 4 because the experimental evidence is persuasive and the mathematical description is correct, but the score is tempered by legitimate concerns about missing ablations for key components, limited reporting on scalability/hyperparameters/statistical significance, and restricted context modelling which hinder full confidence and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 showing NBT matches or outperforms lexicon-based models and benefits from semantically specialised vectors. However, there is no theoretical analysis of the novel architecture or objective, key components (gating, candidate-pair training) lack independent ablations, and scalability/complexity (per-candidate-pair training/memory) and hyperparameters are not reported. Experimental methodology is also incomplete by my standards: only a small set of baselines (one delexicalisation model ± lexicon and Wen et al.) are used, and no statistical significance testing is reported, so some claims about robustness remain insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's central claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments showing NBT matches or outperforms delexicalisation baselines (and exceeds them without semantic lexicons), with strong gains from Paragram-SL999 on noisy DSTC2 ASR output. Mathematical content is minimal but correct for the neural architecture; experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines (including lexicon-augmented models) and realistic noisy data, which I value. Minor concerns remain: context modelling is limited to last system acts (Markovian) and hyperparameter/reporting details and code release are not mentioned, which hinders reproducibility. These issues are not fatal to the technical claims but prevent a full, flawless endorsement.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents solid empirical support across relevant datasets and compares to strong baselines, and they also agree reproducibility details and deeper ablation/sensitivity studies are lacking. Given the strength of the empirical results and clear acknowledgement of limitations, but penalized for missing hyperparameters, code, and broader ablations, a score of 4 reflects that the contribution is strong and useful while not fully comprehensive for replication and analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on two relevant datasets (DSTC2 with ASR noise and WOZ 2.0) and compares to both delexicalisation-based and recent end-to-end trackers, which supports the main empirical claims. However, key ablations are missing (no independent analysis of gating, candidate-pair training regime, or use of specialised vectors), hyperparameters and code are not reported so reproducibility is weak, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing. The authors do note some limitations (Markovian context) but overall the empirical analysis is adequate yet lacking the thorough sensitivity and reproducibility studies I would expect.

reviewer_b.justification: Empirically the paper supports its main claims: evaluations on DSTC2 (noisy ASR) and WOZ 2.0 with comparisons to delexicalisation baselines (with and without semantic dictionaries) and a recent model (Wen et al.) demonstrate the claimed robustness, and the benefit of specialised embeddings (Paragram-SL999) is explicitly shown. Related work is reported as thorough and limitations (Markovian, last-act-only context) are acknowledged. However, reproducibility is weak (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported) and ablation/sensitivity analyses appear limited to encoder variants and embeddings rather than broader hyperparameter or context-window studies, so the experimental coverage is solid but missing some important details for full verification.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a useful, practical advance by removing hand-crafted SLU lexicons and leveraging pre-trained, specialized embeddings to improve dialogue state tracking, with solid empirical gains on realistic noisy data. They also concur the method composes existing neural components rather than offering a deep theoretical breakthrough; however, the clear empirical benefit and data-driven framing make the contribution a non-trivial and valuable extension for the field, meriting a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a moderate novelty: it removes hand-crafted SLU lexicons by reasoning over pre-trained word vectors in a per-candidate slot-value Neural Belief Tracker and shows benefits from semantically specialised vectors (Paragram-SL999). However, the approach largely composes existing elements (pretrained embeddings, DNN/CNN encoders, per-pair binary decisions, simple Markovian context modelling) rather than introducing a fundamentally new formulation or theory. The authors do clearly differentiate from prior delexicalisation and lexicon-augmented baselines and demonstrate empirical gains on DSTC2/WOZ2.0, but the contribution reads as a practical/engineering advance rather than a highly original conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable, practically useful departure from prior delexicalisation-based trackers by using pre-trained distributed word vectors to compose utterance/context representations and making per slot-value binary decisions—removing the need for hand-crafted SLU lexicons and slot-specific parameters. The use of semantically specialised embeddings (Paragram-SL999) and empirical robustness to noisy ASR further differentiate it from earlier work. While the architecture builds on existing neural encoders (DNN/CNN) and gating ideas, the data-driven framing and demonstrated gains on realistic noisy DSTC2 data constitute a clear, non-trivial extension that would inform practitioners.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against appropriate, strong baselines on the same datasets and metrics and presents fair, honest comparisons. I score a 4 because while the evaluation is solid and the paper analyses important factors (e.g., specialised word vectors, ASR noise), it lacks statistical significance testing and broader ablation/coverage of more recent baselines, warranting a slight penalty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 splits and reports joint-goal and request accuracies, comparing against the delexicalisation model of Henderson et al. (2014), that model augmented with a task-specific semantic dictionary, and the Wen et al. (2017) tracker — meeting the expectation of multiple strong baselines. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors honestly note when prior methods match or exceed performance (e.g. when lexical resources are provided, and weaker gains on WOZ 2.0). I penalise the work slightly because the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or broader recent baselines and ablation-driven analysis of how each novel component contributes relative to prior methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines including the delexicalisation model of Henderson et al. (2014) both with and without a task-specific semantic dictionary, and the model of Wen et al. (2017), evaluated on the same DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 data and metrics (joint goal and request accuracy). Comparisons are presented fairly (same datasets/metrics) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior lexicon-augmented methods match or beat variants, while demonstrating NBT’s particular advantage on noisy ASR data and analysing the role of specialised word vectors — all of which indicate comprehensive and relevant prior-work coverage from an applied DST perspective.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and will influence practitioners—particularly by removing hand-crafted SLU lexicons and showing robustness on noisy ASR with semantically specialised vectors. However, they also note methodological limitations (per-candidate training, limited context modelling, and missing scalability/hyperparameter details) that limit its transformative impact. Given its clear practical benefits and likely adoption despite these constraints, a moderate-high impact score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem in dialogue state tracking by removing reliance on hand-crafted SLU lexicons and showing a data-driven Neural Belief Tracker that matches or outperforms lexicon-augmented baselines (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0). From my methodological perspective the work is useful rather than transformative: demonstrating clear benefits of semantically specialised pre-trained vectors (notably on noisy ASR DSTC2) should influence practitioners, but the approach’s per-candidate-pair training regime, limited context modelling, and lack of reported scalability/hyperparameter details reduce its immediate likelihood of radically changing how researchers design trackers. It does open follow-up work (better context models, efficiency analyses, ablations of gating and candidate generation) but those are incremental extensions rather than new paradigms.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem in DST by removing reliance on hand-crafted SLU lexicons and slot-specific parameters, which materially reduces engineering effort for deployed systems. Empirically it demonstrates strong, and particularly valuable, robustness gains on noisy ASR data (DSTC2) and shows that semantically specialised vectors (Paragram‑SL999) substantially improve belief tracking performance — exactly the kind of result that will influence practitioners. The approach is data-driven and likely to be adopted or extended in real systems, though limitations in context modelling (only last system act) and weaker gains on clean WOZ 2.0 keep it from being fully transformative.

main_claims: Propose the Neural Belief Tracker (NBT), a data-driven belief tracking framework that reasons over pre-trained word vectors to compose distributed representations of user utterances and dialogue context.

NBT overcomes the need for hand-crafted SLU lexicons or expensive slot/value-specific parameters, matching state-of-the-art models that use semantic lexicons and outperforming them when such lexicons are not provided.
Show that semantically specialised word vectors (Paragram-SL999) substantially improve belief tracking performance, especially on noisy ASR data.
_summary.methodology: NBT generates one training example per candidate slot-value pair and uses neural encoders (NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN variants) to compose pre-trained word vectors into utterance and context representations, using gating based on the last system act and making binary decisions per candidate pair trained with Adam and cross-entropy loss.
baselines: Delexicalisation-based model of Henderson et al. (2014) (and same model augmented with task-specific semantic dictionary)
Belief tracking model of Wen et al. (2017)
limitations: Context modelling is Markovian and only considers the last system acts (system request or confirm).
Performance gains from pre-trained vectors are weaker on the clean, large WOZ 2.0 dataset (i.e. gains are particularly strong on noisy DSTC2 but weaker on WOZ 2.0).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue state tracking (spoken dialogue systems)

reviewer_a.name: Theory-Focused Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and NLP with a track record of work on formal modelling and theoretical analysis of learning algorithms for language.
traits: Penalises papers that lack formal justification or theoretical analysis of the proposed architecture or learning objective (will lower score if no discussion of capacity, generalisation bounds, or principled motivation beyond engineering intuition).
Requires comprehensive ablation studies for all novel architectural components (will deduct points if gating, candidate-pair generation, or use of specialised vectors are not ablated independently).
Penalises scalability/efficiency omissions (will lower score if there is no analysis of computational/memory cost and training/inference complexity, especially for per-candidate-pair training).

Demands at least three strong baselines including both lexicon-based and recent end-to-end trackers and expects statistical significance testing (will downgrade if baselines are weak or tests are missing).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I scrutinise papers for principled motivation, theoretical grounding, and careful ablations; I reward clear analyses of why a method should work and penalise engineering-only claims without such analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Dialogue Systems Practitioner
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist with years of experience building and evaluating spoken dialogue systems and DST modules for realistic, noisy ASR environments.
traits: Rewards papers demonstrating improved robustness on realistic noisy ASR data (will raise score if experiments show clear gains on ASR outputs).
Favors practical, data-driven approaches that avoid hand-crafted resources (will reward removal of SLU lexicons and learned shared parameters over slot/value-specific ones).
Values strong empirical comparisons and use of pretrained embeddings that lead to measurable gains (will upscore work that shows clear benefits from specialised vectors like Paragram-SL999).
Prefers methods that are likely to be deployable and reproduceable (will penalise only lightly for missing theoretical analysis if code/datasets or clear experimental protocols are provided).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I focus on empirical impact and real-world applicability: methods that demonstrably improve performance in realistic settings and reduce engineering effort get my strong support, even without deep theoretical analysis.
